title: A Letter to Them, Today
subtitle: A journal practice for writing to the person you have lost
excerpt: A short journal practice for writing directly to the person you are grieving. Not a writing exercise or a therapy tool in the clinical sense. A direct letter, today, to them. It holds whatever needs to be said.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Letter-writing in grief is a widely used practice across many grief traditions and therapeutic frameworks, including narrative therapy and continuing-bonds approaches to grief.

content.text: A direct-letter practice for grief. Write to them, in the present tense, as if they can hear you. The practice is used in many grief traditions and does not require a belief in any particular spiritual continuity. It requires only a pen and the willingness to say what has not been said.
What has happened recently that you would have told them?
Write this first. It gets you into their company before you write the harder things.

Write to them directly. Write whatever needs to be said. There is no correct content. You might tell them what you miss. You might tell them what is hard without them. You might ask them a question. You might just report the ordinary details of the week.
Is there anything unsaid that you have been carrying?
Write it. The letter doesn't have to be shown to anyone. The page holds it.
End the letter however feels right.
You do not need to find a perfect closing line. With love is fine. With grief is fine. I miss you is fine.
When you are done, read it back once. Then put it away, or keep it. The practice is the writing, not what you do with the page.

Written for homemade.education. Letter-writing to the deceased is found in grief traditions worldwide and in continuing-bonds grief therapy. The continuing-bonds model, developed in the 1990s, holds that staying connected to the person who died is a normal part of grief.
